raw_case:
Silent Signals: Unveiling Cochlear Nerve Deficiency in a Child Through Imaging
Cochlear nerve deficiency (CND) is a rare cause of congenital sensorineural hearing loss, often leading to serious communication challenges if not diagnosed and treated early. We present this case of a three-year-old male child with congenital hearing loss and sensory aphasia. Despite normal cochlear anatomy on computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed the absence of the cochlear nerve on the right side. The diagnosis of right-sided CND was confirmed, and the patient was advised to undergo cochlear implantation and speech therapy. This case underscores the importance of timely imaging, particularly using MRI with high-resolution sequences, in diagnosing CND and facilitating early intervention to improve auditory and developmental outcomes.

Introduction

Approximately 20% of patients with congenital sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) exhibit radiographic anomalies in the inner ear. A wide range of pathologies can be responsible for these abnormalities [1]. The terms cochlear nerve deficiency (CND) or cochlear nerve (CN) hypoplasia or aplasia refer to a condition where the CN is either underdeveloped or completely absent. Based on radiological findings, these malformations have been estimated to be present in 0.8% to 1.8% of profoundly deaf children.Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is required to assess whether the nerve is defective, though it is often difficult to differentiate between hypoplasia and aplasia due to resolution limitations, especially when the internal auditory canal (IAC) is narrow [2]. However, a missing or dysfunctional cochlear branch of the vestibulocochlear nerve (VCN) could be the reason for congenital deafness and can be assessed with sub-millimetric T2-weighted gradient echo imaging. This imaging technique enables clear visualization of various cranial nerves and their branches within the internal auditory canal and cerebellopontine angle. Another effective method for identifying nerves within the IAC is axial three-dimensional Fourier transformation-constructive interference in steady state (3DFT-CISS) sequence, which facilitates identification of the cochlear branch, inferior vestibular branch, and superior vestibular branch of the VCN [1,3].However, even if the MRI reveals CN aplasia, a full battery of audiometric tests needs to be conducted to rule out other etiological factors before arriving at the final diagnosis.Here, we describe the case of one such child, highlighting the role of radiological investigation in cementing the diagnosis.

Case presentation

This case is of a three-year-old male child who presented to the pediatric outpatient department with congenital hearing loss and sensory aphasia. The mother first noticed the condition when the child was six months old as he did not respond to loud sounds, particularly on the right side. As the child grew older, he failed to respond to his name or follow commands, and his parents observed that he was unable to vocalize words like other children in his age group.The child was his parents' firstborn and was delivered at term via lower segment cesarean section (LSCS) due to being large for gestational age (LGA). He cried immediately after birth, and there was no requirement for a Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU) admission for any perinatal complications. His immunizations were up to date according to the recommended schedule. The mother had not observed any delays in his motor or fine sensory development.Family history revealed that the parents’ marriage was non-consanguineous. The mother also suffers from congenital hearing loss and aphasia but did not undergo any investigations to identify the cause for the same. The father, though unable to speak since childhood, has normal hearing. Although syndromes like Usher, Wolfram, and Pendred syndrome are associated with congenital hearing loss, in this case, the child presented solely with bilateral SNHL with sensory aphasia and no other abnormalities, indicating a likely non-syndromic etiology.For evaluating the child’s hearing loss, a Brainstem Evoked Response Audiometry (BERA) was performed at another hospital and it indicated bilateral hearing loss (profound on the right side, severe on the left side).High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the temporal bone was done in November 2023 using the Philips Ingenuity 128-slice CT scanner (Philips Healthcare, Amsterdam, Netherlands). It revealed a normal cochlea with no structural abnormalities of the inner ear (Figures 1A-1E).Plain MRI of the temporal bone was done in February 2024 using the Siemens 3 Tesla MAGNETOM Vida MRI (Siemens Healthineers, Erlangen, Germany) with thin T1-weighted images and thin T2-weighted images in axial and coronal planes and a Constructive Interference in Steady State (CISS) sequence. On the right side, the cochlear division of the VCN was not seen in the antero-inferior quadrant, suggestive of CND (either marked hypoplasia or agenesis) (Figure 2).However, on the left side, the internal auditory canal showed normal appearance of the cochlear division of VCN in the antero-inferior quadrant (with a calibre larger than the adjacent facial nerve) (Figure 3).Bilateral internal auditory canals were normal (Figure 4).Based on this, a diagnosis of right CND was made. The parents were counselled regarding cochlear implants followed by appropriate speech therapy.

Discussion

An MRI of the temporal bone is a crucial diagnostic tool for cases of congenital profound sensorineural deafness, as it is the only test capable of evaluating the structure of the VCN and its branches [4,5].High resolution and excellent soft tissue/fluid contrast are offered by the standard IAC protocol that uses T2-weighted gradient echo or turbo gradient-spin-echo sequences with sub-millimeter inter-slice spacing and slice thickness [6]. In the present case report, CISS imaging was crucial to confirm the absence of the cochlear nerve on the right side. Additionally, imaging can be done in the axial plane, using either direct acquisition or multi-planar reconstruction to obtain bilateral parasagittal images perpendicular to the nerves. While the latter results in longer scan periods, it offers higher resolution [7].In a study done by Clemmens et al. in 2013, 128 children with unilateral SNHL underwent a high-resolution MRI study [8]. The CN's diameter, area, and signal intensity were assessed and compared to the ipsilateral facial nerve. CND was found in 26% of children with unilateral SNHL, with a higher occurrence in those with profound SNHL.In a study conducted by Nakano et al., 170 children with congenital SNHL were evaluated using CT scans [9]. CND was defined as stenosis of the bony cochlear nerve canal (BCNC), IAC, or both. CND was detected in 50% of the cases with unilateral SNHL compared to 5.3% of cases with bilateral SNHL. The majority of the CND cases were associated with syndromes or cochlear malformations. However, the cochlear anatomy was normal in the present case and unilateral CN agenesis was verified on the MRI scan.While we couldn’t delineate any genetic syndrome associated with the unilateral SNHL in this case, the possibility of additional symptoms and signs emerging over time remains. Therefore, regular screening and follow-up are essential. In their review, Peng et al. observed that better auditory outcomes in these patients are associated with both the presence of a cochlear nerve on the MRI study and the absence of concomitant medical syndromes [10].

Conclusions

Cochlear nerve agenesis or hypoplasia is a rare condition and a known cause of SNHL, more frequently associated with unilateral than bilateral SNHL. Although it has been studied extensively, diagnosing this condition can be challenging due to the difficulties associated with imaging of the narrow IAC, which is often associated with CND. Hence, in addition to audiometric tests and HRCT of the temporal bone, an MRI is also essential to screen patients presenting with congenital SNHL to evaluate the CN for any abnormalities. Furthermore, for patients with a family history of congenital deafness, early screening is important as it can enhance auditory outcomes and mitigate the risk of subsequent developmental anomalies like aphasia.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 3-year-old male, firstborn child.
- **Chief Complaint:** Congenital hearing loss and sensory aphasia.
- **History of Present Illness:** Mother observed absence of response to loud sounds starting at six months of age, inability to respond to his name, failure to follow commands, and delayed speech development compared to peers.
- **Past Medical History:** None reported.
- **Personal History:** Born via lower segment cesarean section (LSCS) due to being large for gestational age (LGA), cried immediately after birth, no NICU admission, and immunizations up to date.
- **Family History:** Non-consanguineous marriage. Mother has congenital hearing loss and aphasia (cause undiagnosed). Father has normal hearing but is non-verbal since childhood. No known syndromic associations such as Usher, Wolfram, or Pendred syndromes.
- **Physical Examination:** None specified.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - **Audiometry:** Brainstem Evoked Response Audiometry (BERA) revealed bilateral SNHL (profound on the right, severe on the left).
  - **High-Resolution CT:** Normal cochlear and inner ear anatomy.
  - **MRI (using CISS sequence):** Absence of cochlear nerve on the right side, confirming right-sided cochlear nerve deficiency (CND). Left side cochlear nerve appeared normal. Internal auditory canals were normal bilaterally.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Suspicions Based on Symptomatology:**
   - Congenital sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) was suspected due to the child’s hearing and speech deficits starting from infancy.
   - The family history raised the possibility of an inherited or syndromic form of hearing impairment but no syndromic features or systemic abnormalities were identified.

2. **Rationale for Diagnostic Tests:**
   - **Audiometric Tests:** Confirmed the degree and laterality of hearing loss (profound on the right and severe on the left).
   - **High-Resolution CT:** Ruled out structural anomalies in the cochlea or other inner ear structures.
   - **MRI (CISS sequence):** Specifically visualized the cochlear nerve, revealing unilateral right-sided CND (absence of cochlear nerve). This imaging modality was key in ruling out other causes of SNHL.

3. **Exclusion of Other Differential Diagnoses:**
   - Syndromic SNHL was excluded due to lack of associated abnormalities.
   - Cochlear malformations or anatomical defects were excluded by normal HRCT findings.
   - Functional causes of SNHL unrelated to cochlear nerve pathology were excluded through MRI findings.
   - Other conditions like auditory neuropathy spectrum disorder were not evident based on presented data.

4. **Final Consolidation:**
   - Unilateral right-sided cochlear nerve deficiency was identified as the cause of the profound hearing loss on the right side, with coexisting non-syndromic SNHL on the left.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The patient was diagnosed with **unilateral right-sided cochlear nerve deficiency (CND), contributing to profound sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) on the right and severe non-syndromic SNHL on the left**. Early cochlear implantation and speech therapy were recommended.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Cochlear nerve deficiency (CND) on the right side with bilateral sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL).

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 3-year-old boy with congenital hearing loss and sensory aphasia. The main issues here are the hearing loss and the speech problems. Let me start by breaking down the information provided.

First, the chief complaints are congenital hearing loss and sensory aphasia. The mother noticed no response to loud sounds from six months, which is a key point. Also, he can't respond to his name or follow commands, and his speech is delayed. So, the hearing loss seems significant and early-onset.

Looking at the past medical history, he was born via C-section because he was large for gestational age, but he cried immediately, no NICU stay. Immunizations are up to date, so probably no infections or complications post-birth that could contribute. Family history is interesting: mother has congenital hearing loss and aphasia (undiagnosed cause), father is non-verbal but with normal hearing. Parents are non-consanguineous, but there's a family history of similar issues. Syndromic associations like Usher, Wolfram, or Pendred are denied, so maybe a genetic cause that's non-syndromic?

Ancillary tests: BERA shows bilateral sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL)—profound on right, severe on left. High-resolution CT shows normal cochlear and inner ear anatomy, so structural issues in the cochlea are ruled out. MRI with CISS sequence found absence of the cochlear nerve on the right (cochlear nerve deficiency, CND) and normal left cochlear nerve. Internal auditory canals are normal bilaterally.

Wait, so the cochlear nerve is absent on the right, which explains the profound SNHL there. On the left, the nerve is normal, but the hearing loss is severe. But how does that fit? Maybe the left side has another issue, like a genetic component affecting the nerve function even if the structure is normal. The CT being normal suggests the cochlea itself is okay, so the problem is neural, either the nerve or the central pathways.

The family history is crucial. Mother has congenital hearing loss and aphasia; father is non-verbal but with normal hearing. The father being non-verbal but with normal hearing is puzzling—maybe a separate genetic condition affecting speech, like aphasia unrelated to hearing? But the child has both hearing loss and aphasia, which could be linked.

Given that the mother and child both have hearing loss and aphasia, this points towards a hereditary cause. Since the parents are non-consanguineous, but both have some communication issues, maybe an autosomal dominant or X-linked condition. However, the father's normal hearing but non-verbal status complicates things. Alternatively, maybe the mother has a genetic hearing loss, and the father contributes a different mutation affecting speech, but that's less likely.

The cochlear nerve deficiency on the right is a structural anomaly. But the left side's nerve is normal, yet there's severe hearing loss. Could this be a bilateral issue with one side having a structural absence and the other a functional problem? Alternatively, maybe the left side has a different type of neural issue not visible on imaging. Or perhaps a genetic mutation affecting both the cochlear nerve (on the right) and the function of the auditory nerve on the left.

Common genetic causes of congenital SNHL include connexin mutations (GJB2/GJB6), which are the most common. But those typically present with bilateral SNHL and normal imaging. However, in this case, there's a structural nerve abnormality on the right. So maybe a combination of genetic factors and structural issues?

Wait, but the CT and MRI showed normal cochlea and internal auditory canals. The absence of the cochlear nerve on the right is CND. Cochlear nerve deficiency can be isolated or part of a syndrome. Since the family history includes hearing loss and aphasia in the mother, and the father is non-verbal, could this be a genetic syndrome affecting both auditory nerves and language development?

Alternatively, maybe the left severe SNHL is due to a genetic cause, while the right CND is a separate structural anomaly. But how likely is that? Or perhaps the CND is part of a genetic condition. Some genetic mutations can cause cochlear nerve hypoplasia/aplasia. For example, mutations in genes like SLC17A8, which is associated with auditory neuropathy. However, auditory neuropathy typically shows present OAE but absent ABR, which might align with the BERA findings here (since BERA is the same as ABR). If the cochlear nerve is absent, the ABR would be abnormal.

But in this case, the right cochlear nerve is absent (CND), leading to profound SNHL. The left has a normal cochlear nerve but severe SNHL. If the left's hearing loss is sensorineural, perhaps due to a genetic mutation affecting the hair cells or the nerve function. Since the CT and MRI are normal on the left, maybe a genetic cause like GJB2 mutation. However, GJB2-related hearing loss usually has normal imaging. Alternatively, a different genetic cause that also affects the cochlear nerve on the right.

The family history is pointing towards a genetic origin. The mother has congenital hearing loss and aphasia, which could be the same condition. The father's non-verbal status might be a separate issue, but perhaps related. Since the parents are non-consanguineous, autosomal dominant or X-linked is possible. For example, if the mother has an autosomal dominant condition, she could pass it to the child. However, the father's non-verbal status complicates unless it's a de novo mutation or another gene.

Wait, another angle: the child has cochlear nerve deficiency on the right and severe SNHL on the left. The presence of CND can be associated with certain genetic syndromes or mutations. For example, CHD7 mutations (CHARGE syndrome) can cause inner ear anomalies, but in this case, the cochlea is normal. Alternatively, SOX10 mutations can cause Waardenburg syndrome type 4C or PCWH (peripheral demyelinating neuropathy, central dysmyelinating leukodystrophy, Waardenburg syndrome, and Hirschsprung disease), which can include hearing loss. But there's no mention of other features like pigment changes or Hirschsprung here.

Alternatively, a mutation in a gene involved in cochlear nerve development. For example, Neurotrophic tyrosine kinase receptor type 1 (NTRK1) mutations, but that's associated with congenital insensitivity to pain. Not sure. Maybe a gene like SLC29A3, but again, not directly linked.

Another thought: since the cochlear nerve is absent on the right, that's a congenital anomaly. The left side's severe SNHL could be due to a different mechanism, but given the family history, perhaps a genetic predisposition. So, the combination of structural (CND) and genetic factors.

But the MRI shows normal internal auditory canals, which sometimes are narrowed in CND. Here, they're normal, so the CND is isolated. The presence of CND in the context of family history of hearing loss and aphasia suggests a possible genetic link. For example, some genes are involved in both cochlear nerve development and auditory function.

Wait, the mother has congenital hearing loss and aphasia, undiagnosed. The father is non-verbal since childhood but with normal hearing. So, the child inherited hearing loss from the mother and perhaps a speech disorder from the father? But the child's aphasia might be secondary to the hearing loss. Sensory aphasia in a child with profound hearing loss could be due to the inability to process auditory input, leading to language delay. However, the term "sensory aphasia" is more specific and could indicate a central processing issue, not just deafness. But in children, severe hearing loss can mimic aphasia because they can't acquire language without auditory input.

Alternatively, the aphasia could be part of a broader neurological condition. However, the imaging (MRI) didn't show any central abnormalities, only the cochlear nerve issue. So, maybe the aphasia is secondary to the hearing loss. But the mother also has aphasia and hearing loss. If her aphasia is due to the same cause, perhaps a genetic syndrome that affects both hearing and language centers in the brain.

Putting it all together: the child has bilateral SNHL (right profound due to CND, left severe possibly genetic), and a family history of hearing loss and language disorders. The most likely diagnosis is a genetic sensorineural hearing loss with cochlear nerve deficiency. Given the autosomal dominant inheritance suggested by the mother's history, possible genes could include those associated with auditory neuropathy or nerve malformations.

However, another angle is that the right CND is an isolated structural anomaly, and the left hearing loss is due to a separate genetic cause inherited from the mother. But the combination of both in the same patient seems less likely. More plausible is a single underlying genetic cause leading to both the CND and the severe SNHL on the left.

Given that connexin 26 (GJB2) mutations are the most common cause of non-syndromic SNHL, but they usually have normal imaging. However, in this case, there's a structural anomaly. So maybe a different gene. For example, a mutation in a gene involved in both cochlear development and auditory nerve function.

Alternatively, since the left cochlear nerve is present but there's severe SNHL, maybe a genetic mutation affecting the hair cells or the synapse (auditory neuropathy spectrum disorder). But BERA would show abnormal waveforms in that case. Wait, BERA here shows bilateral SNHL, which in the presence of cochlear nerve deficiency on the right could explain the profound loss there, and perhaps a different mechanism on the left.

But given the family history and the imaging findings, the most likely diagnosis is genetic sensorineural hearing loss with cochlear nerve deficiency. The combination of a structural anomaly (CND) and genetic predisposition (family history) points towards a genetic syndrome, possibly autosomal dominant, given the mother's similar issues.

However, without syndromic features mentioned, it's more likely non-syndromic genetic hearing loss. But non-syndromic hearing loss with CND? Hmm. Some studies have linked certain genes to cochlear nerve anomalies. For example, mutations in the PAX3 gene (associated with Waardenburg syndrome) can cause hearing loss with nerve anomalies, but again, other features would be expected.

Alternatively, a de novo mutation in a gene not previously linked, but that's speculative. The key here is the combination of family history and imaging findings.

In conclusion, the child has bilateral sensorineural hearing loss with right cochlear nerve deficiency. Given the family history of hearing loss and aphasia in the mother and non-verbal father, the diagnosis is likely a genetic form of non-syndromic hearing loss, possibly autosomal dominant, with variable expressivity. The cochlear nerve deficiency on the right could be part of this genetic condition. However, given the structural anomaly and the genetic component, the final diagnosis would be Cochlear Nerve Deficiency (right) with underlying Genetic Sensorineural Hearing Loss. But since the MRI confirms CND, and the family history suggests a hereditary component, perhaps the primary diagnosis is Cochlear Nerve Deficiency with a genetic etiology. But I need to check if CND can be part of a genetic disorder.

Upon checking, some genetic conditions can present with CND. For example, mutations in genes like SOX10 (associated with Waardenburg syndrome type IV and PCWH) can cause cochlear nerve hypoplasia. However, without other features of these syndromes, it's hard to say. Given that the family has isolated hearing loss and aphasia, and the child has CND, the most precise diagnosis is bilateral sensorineural hearing loss due to right cochlear nerve deficiency and a genetic mutation (like GJB2 or another gene) causing the left-sided severe SNHL. But given the imaging and test results, the primary structural issue is the right CND. However, the left ear's severe SNHL with normal structure suggests a different mechanism, possibly genetic.

Wait, but the mother has congenital hearing loss and aphasia. If the mother's hearing loss is also due to CND, but that wasn't evaluated. If the mother's hearing loss is genetic, the child could have inherited that, plus the CND. But without info on the mother's imaging, it's speculative.

Alternatively, the child's hearing loss is due to the combination of CND (right) and genetic SNHL (left). But the family history suggests a hereditary component affecting both hearing and speech. The father's non-verbal status with normal hearing is a bit confusing. Maybe the father has a developmental language disorder unrelated to hearing, which the child also inherited, compounding the language delay from hearing loss.

But the primary issue here is the congenital hearing loss. The final diagnosis should reflect the structural anomaly (CND) and the genetic component. However, in the absence of a specific syndrome, the diagnosis would be Cochlear Nerve Deficiency (right) and Genetic Sensorineural Hearing Loss (left), but how to combine these? Alternatively, the right CND could be part of a genetic disorder affecting the auditory nerve.

But the MRI shows normal internal auditory canals. CND with normal IACs is possible. In some cases, CND can occur with normal bony canals because the nerve itself is absent or hypoplastic.

Considering all this, the most accurate diagnosis based on the provided data is Bilateral Sensorineural Hearing Loss (right profound due to cochlear nerve deficiency, left severe with possible genetic etiology) with a family history suggestive of hereditary hearing loss. But the question is to provide a single diagnosis. The MRI confirms right cochlear nerve deficiency, which is a congenital anomaly. However, the left side's severe SNHL with normal anatomy might be due to a genetic cause. So the final diagnosis would be Right Cochlear Nerve Deficiency and Genetic Sensorineural Hearing Loss. But the answer needs to be a single diagnosis.

Alternatively, the presence of a genetic mutation causing SNHL in the left ear and CND on the right. However, this is speculative without genetic testing. The most evidence-based diagnosis is Cochlear Nerve Deficiency (right) and Genetic Sensorineural Hearing Loss (left). But since the answer requires a single diagnosis, perhaps the primary structural diagnosis is Cochlear Nerve Deficiency, and the genetic component is a separate diagnosis. But the case summary doesn't mention genetic testing results.

Alternatively, since the family history suggests autosomal dominant inheritance with variable expressivity, and the child has both CND and SNHL, the diagnosis could be a genetic syndrome with cochlear nerve deficiency and SNHL. However, without specific syndrome features, it's non-syndromic. Therefore, the most accurate diagnosis is Bilateral Sensorineural Hearing Loss (right cochlear nerve deficiency, left severe) due to a genetic etiology. But how to phrase it concisely?

Alternatively, the presence of cochlear nerve deficiency and a family history of hearing loss points to a diagnosis of Hereditary Hearing Loss with Cochlear Nerve Aplasia. But I think the key here is the imaging finding of CND. The answer should include that. The case meets criteria for Cochlear Nerve Deficiency (right) and possible genetic hearing loss on the left, but the exact etiology without genetic testing is unclear. However, the presence of family history strongly suggests a genetic component. So, the final diagnosis is likely Genetic Sensorineural Hearing Loss with Cochlear Nerve Deficiency. But the format requires a concise answer. The primary structural anomaly is CND, but the left ear's SNHL might be due to a genetic cause. The family history suggests genetic. But the CT is normal, so cochlea is fine. The MRI shows CND on the right, so that's a structural cause for the right-sided hearing loss. results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with congenital bilateral sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL)—profound on the right and severe on the left—and sensory aphasia. Imaging reveals right-sided cochlear nerve deficiency (CND) with normal left cochlear nerve and cochlear anatomy.  
<step 2> The family history includes a mother with congenital hearing loss/aphasia and a non-verbal father with normal hearing, strongly suggesting a genetic predisposition, though no syndromic features are reported.  
<step 3> Right-sided CND explains the profound SNHL on that side. The left-sided severe SNHL, despite normal cochlear nerve anatomy, points to a genetic etiology, given the familial pattern of hearing and language disorders.  
<step 4> Non-syndromic genetic causes (e.g., GJB2 mutations) typically present with normal imaging, but the coexistence of structural (CND) and functional (left SNHL) abnormalities suggests a hereditary condition affecting both neural development and auditory function.  
<step 5> The combination of structural CND and familial SNHL/aphasia aligns with a diagnosis of hereditary hearing loss with variable expressivity, incorporating both cochlear nerve malformation and genetic auditory dysfunction.

### Answer:
Hereditary Sensorineural Hearing Loss with Right Cochlear Nerve Deficiency

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with congenital bilateral sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL)—profound on the right and severe on the left—and sensory aphasia. Imaging reveals right-sided cochlear nerve deficiency (CND) with normal left cochlear nerve and cochlear anatomy.

The family history includes a mother with congenital hearing loss/aphasia and a non-verbal father with normal hearing, strongly suggesting a genetic predisposition, though no syndromic features are reported.

Right-sided CND explains the profound SNHL on that side. The left-sided severe SNHL, despite normal cochlear nerve anatomy, points to a genetic etiology, given the familial pattern of hearing and language disorders.

Non-syndromic genetic causes (e.g., GJB2 mutations) typically present with normal imaging, but the coexistence of structural (CND) and functional (left SNHL) abnormalities suggests a hereditary condition affecting both neural development and auditory function.

The combination of structural CND and familial SNHL/aphasia aligns with a diagnosis of hereditary hearing loss with variable expressivity, incorporating both cochlear nerve malformation and genetic auditory dysfunction.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Congenital sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) was suspected due to the child's hearing and speech deficits starting from infancy, with no identified syndromic features or systemic abnormalities.
Step 2: Audiometric tests confirmed the degree and laterality of hearing loss (profound on the right and severe on the left).
Step 3: High-resolution CT ruled out structural anomalies in the cochlea or other inner ear structures.
Step 4: MRI (CISS sequence) specifically visualized the cochlear nerve, revealing unilateral right-sided CND (absence of cochlear nerve).
Step 5: Syndromic SNHL was excluded due to the lack of associated abnormalities.
Step 6: Cochlear malformations or anatomical defects were excluded by normal HRCT findings.
Step 7: Functional causes of SNHL unrelated to cochlear nerve pathology were excluded through MRI findings.
Step 8: Other conditions like auditory neuropathy spectrum disorder were not evident based on presented data.

Step 9: The patient was diagnosed with unilateral right-sided cochlear nerve deficiency (CND), contributing to profound sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) on the right and severe non-syndromic SNHL on the left.
Step 10: Early cochlear implantation and speech therapy were recommended.
